children.name: Accipiter
children.description: Goshawks and sparrowhawks — the archetypal bird-hunting hawks, with short rounded wings and long tails adapted for rapid manoeuvre through woodland. The Northern Goshawk is the apex forest raptor of the temperate Northern Hemisphere, capable of overtaking prey at speed through dense trees. The Eurasian Sparrowhawk is the most numerous hawk in Britain and Europe, ambushing small birds at garden feeders and woodland edges in an explosive burst of acceleration. Both species show strong reverse sexual dimorphism — females are dramatically larger than males.

children.name: Accipiter gentilis
children.description: The 'cook's hawk' of medieval falconry, valued above all others for hunting hares and pheasants. Once extirpated from Britain by gamekeepers; now recolonising from escaped falconry birds and deliberate reintroductions.

children.name: Accipiter nisus
children.description: The most abundant raptor in Europe. DDT-related eggshell thinning collapsed populations post-WWII; recovery after the pesticide ban was one of the first demonstrations of successful raptor conservation.

children.name: Accipiter striatus
children.description: The smallest hawk in North America, a dashing bird-hunter of forests and a familiar visitor to backyard feeders where it ambushes small birds.

children.name: Accipiter badius
children.description: A small, widespread sparrowhawk of Africa and southern Asia, a bold hunter of lizards, small birds and insects in open woodland and gardens.

children.name: Buteo
children.description: Buzzards — broad-winged, soaring hawks of open and woodland landscapes. The Common Buzzard is the most abundant and widespread raptor in Europe, its mewing, cat-like call a characteristic sound of the upland and woodland edge. Buzzards are versatile and opportunistic hunters, taking rabbits, voles, earthworms, and carrion depending on season and availability. In North America, 'buzzard' colloquially refers to vultures, while what Europeans call buzzards are known as 'hawks' across the Atlantic.

children.name: Buteo buteo
children.description: The most numerous raptor in Britain. Plumage is highly variable from pale cream to near-black; carpal patches on the underside of the wing are a consistent ID feature. Declined massively due to persecution; recovered strongly since the 1970s.

children.name: Buteo lagopus
children.description: An Arctic-breeding buzzard that winters in open farmland across northern Europe. Distinguished by feathered legs (the source of the name: lagopus = 'hare-foot') and a distinctive black belly patch and white tail base.

children.name: Buteo jamaicensis
children.description: The most familiar and widespread raptor in North America, found from Alaska to Panama. Its scream is used as the default 'raptor sound' in virtually every film and television production, even when the bird shown is an eagle.

children.name: Buteo rufinus
children.description: A large pale buzzard of steppes and semi-deserts from southeastern Europe to Central Asia, often hunting rodents from a hovering position.

children.name: Buteo swainsoni
children.description: A slender prairie hawk that makes one of the longest migrations of any North American raptor, travelling in huge flocks to the pampas of Argentina.

children.name: Aquila
children.description: True eagles — large, powerful raptors of mountains, forests, and open plains. The Golden Eagle is the most widespread eagle in the Northern Hemisphere and one of the best-studied raptors in the world. It holds territories of up to 100 km² and is capable of taking prey up to the size of young red deer and mountain goats. Eagles have been symbols of power across human cultures for millennia — on Roman standards, medieval heraldry, and modern national emblems alike.

children.name: Aquila chrysaetos
children.description: Scotland holds the majority of the UK breeding population; English reintroduction is underway. In Scotland, a Golden Eagle territory in the Highlands can cover 50–80 km². The golden nape feathers are visible at close range and give the bird its name.

children.name: Aquila clanga
children.description: One of Europe's most endangered raptors; fewer than 1,000 pairs remain globally. Breeds in lowland riparian forest in eastern Europe and Russia. Hybridises extensively with Lesser Spotted Eagle where ranges overlap.

children.name: Aquila heliaca
children.description: Heraldic bird of the Habsburg Empire and still on the Czech coat of arms. A conservation success story: central European populations have recovered from near-extinction through nest protection and electrocution prevention on power pylons.

children.name: Aquila rapax
children.description: A widespread but declining eagle of African and South Asian savannas, an opportunistic predator and notorious pirate of other raptors' kills. Now Vulnerable.

children.name: Aquila nipalensis
children.description: A large migratory eagle of Central Asian steppes that winters in Africa and South Asia. Listed as Endangered owing to steep population declines.

children.name: Haliaeetus
children.description: Sea eagles — the largest eagles, specialising in fish, waterbirds, and carrion along coastlines, rivers, and lakes. The White-tailed Eagle is the largest eagle in Europe, with a wingspan exceeding 2.4 metres. It was exterminated from Britain by 1918 through poisoning, shooting, and egg collection, and has since been successfully reintroduced — first to Scotland in the 1970s, then to the Isle of Wight in 2019. The Bald Eagle — national emblem of the United States — recovered dramatically following DDT restrictions and delisting from the Endangered Species Act in 2007.

children.name: Haliaeetus albicilla
children.description: Europe's largest eagle, with a wingspan up to 2.4 m. Extinct in Britain by 1918; reintroduced to Scotland from Norway starting in 1975. Now around 150 pairs breed in Scotland, with birds expanding to Ireland and England.

children.name: Haliaeetus leucocephalus
children.description: The national bird of the United States, its white head and tail not acquired until the fourth or fifth year. DDT caused catastrophic eggshell thinning; after the ban in 1972 and intensive recovery programmes, it was delisted from the Endangered Species Act in 2007.

children.name: Haliaeetus pelagicus
children.description: One of the heaviest eagles in the world, with a massive orange-yellow bill, breeding on the coasts of far-eastern Russia and wintering in Japan. Vulnerable.

children.name: Circus
children.description: Harriers — slender, long-winged, long-tailed hawks that hunt by quartering low over marshland, moorland, and grassland on upswept wings. Their distinctive OWL-like facial disc gives them acute directional hearing for detecting prey in long vegetation. The Hen Harrier is one of the most contentious birds in Britain, where it is intensively persecuted on grouse moors and has been essentially eliminated as a breeding bird from England despite legal protection. Males are ghost-grey, females and immatures are streaky brown.

children.name: Circus cyaneus
children.description: A Red List species in Britain primarily due to illegal killing on driven grouse moors. Males are pale grey with black wing-tips; females are brown with a white rump. Displays spectacular sky-dancing courtship flights.

children.name: Circus aeruginosus
children.description: The largest European harrier, breeding in reedbeds and marshes. Reduced to near-extinction in Britain in the 20th century; now around 400 pairs following reedbed restoration and legal protection.

children.name: Milvus
children.description: Red and Black kites — elegant, long-winged raptors with forked tails and a buoyant, effortless flight. The Red Kite is one of the great conservation success stories of recent decades: nearly exterminated in Britain (with only a small Welsh population surviving into the 20th century), it has been reintroduced to England, Scotland, and Ireland, and now numbers in the thousands. Medieval London was full of kites, which scavenged the streets — a historical parallel to the city kites of modern Nairobi.

children.name: Milvus milvus
children.description: One of Europe's most successful raptor reintroductions. British reintroduction in 1989 (Chilterns and Wales) now accounts for over 10% of the global population. The forked tail and rusty plumage are unmistakable in flight.

children.name: Milvus migrans
children.description: The most numerous raptor in the world. An adaptable scavenger of towns, rubbish dumps, rivers, and farmland. Millions funnel through the Strait of Gibraltar and Bosphorus on migration.

children.name: Pernis
children.description: Honey buzzards — specialised wasp and hornet nest robbers. Despite the name they are more closely related to kites than true buzzards. The European Honey Buzzard spends much of its summer excavating bee and wasp nests from the ground, eating larvae, pupae, and comb — a diet almost unique among raptors. Scale-like feathers around the bill and eyes provide protection from stings. It is one of the latest spring migrants to arrive in Europe, timed to coincide with the establishment of wasp colonies.

children.name: Pernis apivorus
children.description: An enigmatic and variable raptor — plumage ranges from very pale to near-black. A strict long-distance migrant; most European birds winter in west Africa. Remarkably inconspicuous on the breeding ground despite open nest placement.

children.name: Gyps
children.description: Griffon vultures — colonial, cliff-nesting carrion specialists of Africa, Europe, and Asia. Old World vultures evolved flight soaring independently from New World vultures (Cathartidae) and are more closely related to eagles and hawks. Griffon Vultures are the premier bone-cleaners of the Palearctic, descending in dozens on large carcasses and consuming them within hours. Their naked head and neck prevent feather fouling when reaching inside carcasses. Once common across much of Europe, reduced by poisoning and now recovering in southern Europe.

children.name: Gyps fulvus
children.description: Wingspan up to 2.8 m. Locates carcasses by sight and by watching the behaviour of other vultures. Soars on thermals to cover vast distances daily in search of food; may travel over 300 km from the roost to a carcass.

children.name: Aegypius
children.description: The cinereous vulture, one of the largest birds of prey in the world, a massive Old World vulture of Eurasian mountains and steppes.

children.name: Aegypius monachus
children.description: The largest Old World vulture and one of the heaviest flying birds, soaring over the mountains and steppes of Eurasia. Near Threatened.

children.name: Neophron
children.description: The Egyptian vulture, a small migratory Old World vulture famous for using stones as tools to break open eggs.

children.name: Neophron percnopterus
children.description: A small migratory vulture famous for using stones as tools to crack open ostrich eggs. Endangered due to poisoning and persecution.

children.name: Gypaetus
children.description: The bearded vulture or lammergeier, a bone-eating mountain vulture of Eurasia and Africa, unique among raptors in its diet of bone marrow.

children.name: Gypaetus barbatus
children.description: The lammergeier, a spectacular mountain vulture that subsists largely on bone, dropping large bones onto rocks to shatter them. Near Threatened.

children.name: Circaetus
children.description: Snake eagles, raptors specialised in hunting reptiles, with broad heads and bare legs protective against snakebite.

children.name: Circaetus gallicus
children.description: A pale, large-headed eagle that hovers over open country hunting snakes and lizards, migrating from Europe to sub-Saharan Africa.

children.name: Clanga
children.description: Spotted eagles, medium-sized dark eagles of wetlands and forests in Eurasia, several of conservation concern.

children.name: Clanga pomarina
children.description: A forest eagle of eastern Europe that migrates in enormous flocks to southern Africa, hunting small mammals and amphibians in damp meadows.
